; The HbA1c threshold is set to **≥ 9 g%**, which is clinically unreasonable for a diabetes diagnosis. Standard diagnostic criteria use **≥ 6.5 %** (or ≥ 48 mmol/mol). A value of 9 % would indicate markedly uncontrolled diabetes rather than a diagnostic cutoff.; The unit **g%** is not a recognized unit for HbA1c. The decision logic does not enforce the USPSTF‐required 20‑pack‑year smoking history. The “Has Smoking History” expression only checks for an active Condition coded in the “Tobacco User” value set, but it does not verify the quantity (e.g., ≥20 pack‑years) or duration of smoking. Additionally, the recent LDCT check uses an Observation resource; USPSTF guidance typically records screening studies as Procedure resources, though this is a minor modeling issue compared with the missing pack‑year requirement.

; **Age criteria**: The CDS limits eligibility to patients aged 60‑75 years. The lower bound of 60 years excludes a large portion of the recommended screening population.; **Screening interval**: The rule defines “recent screening” as any colorectal cancer screening observation within the past **2 years**. These discrepancies make the implementation clinically unreasonable.

The age criterion ("In Demographic") uses a cutoff of ≥ 30 years, which is not aligned with standard HIV screening guidelines (e.g., CDC recommends at least one HIV test for all individuals aged 13–64 or 15–65). The BMI threshold used to define obesity is set to ≥ 35 kg/m². Clinical guidelines (e.g., USPSTF, ADA) recommend screening adults ≥ 35 years old who are overweight or obese, defined as BMI ≥ 25 kg/m². Using a BMI ≥ 35 kg/m² will exclude many patients who meet guideline criteria, making the rule clinically unreasonable. All other values (age 35‑70, 1‑year glucose/HbA1c test window, recommendation text) are appropriate.

This creates a clinical mismatch: women aged 21‑29 who should be screened according to the guideline are excluded by the logic.
